title: Check yourself
questions.0.q: You forget <meta charset="utf-8">. On your machine everything looks fine. When does it break?
questions.0.options.1: Immediately — the page will not load
questions.0.options.2: When opened elsewhere, where letters fall apart
questions.0.options.3: Only in a browser older than ten years
questions.0.hint: Think about who will see the problem — you on your machine, or somebody else on theirs?
questions.0.explain: Your browser can guess the encoding. Somebody else's cannot. The code works for you and breaks for them. That is the sneakiest kind of bug: you never see it.
questions.1.q: You write <html> with no lang. What do you lose?
questions.1.options.0: Nothing — the page looks exactly the same
questions.1.options.1: The page stops working on a phone
questions.1.options.2: The browser logs a warning to the console
questions.1.options.3: The reader does not know how to pronounce it
questions.1.hint: Nothing does change on screen. Ask who is NOT looking at the screen.
questions.1.explain: Nothing changes on screen, which is exactly why it is easy to skip. But the screen reader speaks your text in the wrong voice, and Google does not know who to show it to.
questions.2.options.0: In <body>, because it shows on screen
questions.2.options.1: In <head>, because it is the page's heading
questions.2.options.3: Anywhere — the browser sorts it out itself
questions.2.hint: There is one rule: does it show on screen, or not?
questions.2.explain: The last answer is sneaky because it is partly true: the browser really does move <h1> from the head into the body. But then your code says one thing and the document is another — it works and it lies.
questions.3.options.0: Declares the document is written in HTML5
questions.3.options.1: Tells the browser which HTML version it reads
questions.3.options.2: Switches the browser into standards mode
questions.3.hint: Two of the answers say almost the same thing. If there are two, both are probably wrong.
questions.3.explain: The first two are the most widespread explanation and they are a myth. Older standards did give this line such a meaning, but no browser has ever used it for anything other than switching the mode.
questions.4.q: You forget </title>. What comes out on the page?
questions.4.options.0: Nothing — the title swallows everything after it
questions.4.options.1: All fine, only the tab name is missing
questions.4.options.2: The browser stops and shows an error message
questions.4.options.3: The heading appears twice — on top and in the body
questions.4.hint: An unclosed tag has no end. So how far does it reach?
questions.4.explain: The element never ends, so it eats everything after it — the body included. That is why an unclosed <title> is the easiest to spot: the page simply disappears.
questions.5.q: You write . What does alt become?
questions.5.options.0: Blue bicycle, exactly as you wrote it
questions.5.options.1: Blue — and bicycle becomes a separate attribute
questions.5.options.2: Empty, because the quotes are missing
questions.5.options.3: The browser refuses to show the image
questions.5.hint: What separates two attributes from each other? The same thing sits in the middle of that text.
questions.5.explain: A space separates attributes. With no quotes there is nothing to show that the two words are one value — half the text vanishes silently, with no error and no warning.
questions.6.q: Which is true of <video src="clip.mp4" controls>?
questions.6.options.0: controls is wrong — it is missing a value
questions.6.options.1: controls has to be written controls="true"
questions.6.options.3: controls is switched on by its name alone
questions.6.hint: Some attributes have nothing to specify. Either they are there or they are not.
questions.6.explain: These are called boolean attributes. Their presence is the switch. Writing "true" is not an error but it is redundant — and a value of "false" will not turn them off.
questions.7.q: You put a password in an HTML comment. Who can read it?
questions.7.options.0: Nobody — comments never reach the browser
questions.7.options.1: Only you, opening the file in an editor
questions.7.options.2: Any visitor, with right-click and View Source
questions.7.options.3: Only programs that read the code automatically
questions.7.hint: A comment is not displayed. But does it travel to the visitor?
questions.7.explain: A comment reaches every visitor along with the rest of the page. That is why OWASP has a dedicated procedure for reviewing comments — people put passwords and addresses in them and then forget.
questions.8.q: You wrap one line in <!-- -->. What happens to that line?
questions.8.options.0: It is removed from the file on the next save
questions.8.options.1: It stays in the file and travels to the visitor
questions.8.options.2: It runs, but is not shown on the screen
questions.8.options.3: The server strips it before sending the page
questions.8.hint: Commented-out code does not run. But is that the same as deleted?
questions.8.explain: Hidden does not mean gone. The line is still in the file and still travels across the network. To the browser it is absent; to the network it is present.
questions.9.q: Why does the browser not show an error for broken HTML?
questions.9.options.0: Because errors in HTML are impossible
questions.9.options.1: Because it only shows them in developer mode
questions.9.options.2: Because it silently repairs code by built-in rules
questions.9.options.3: Because it waits for the validator to find them
questions.9.hint: Remember the wreck. The page did show. Just not the way you expected.
questions.9.explain: It was decided at the start of the web: it matters more that people can publish than that the syntax is flawless. So you always get something that works — but not always the right thing.
